In JLA examples of the perfect with a full short /a/ after the shin, other than those wherein the meaning is clearly "to desire," should be understood as peal.
In some texts (in particular in Syriac) the distinction between 'requesting' and 'asking a question, interrogating' is maintained by distinguishing between the peal and the pael respectively; in others that does not seem to be the case.

entries.end_notes:
Akkadian ša ekalli; see AIOA 97 and , for the precise nuance in Akkadian, S. Arbeli-Rave, Leshonenu 52(1988): 8ff. In some dialects this word apparently referred to what we would call "queen" while mlkh was the "queen mother"; q.v. For extensive discussion of the Hebrew usage ‏נִצְּבָה שֵׁגַל לִימִינְךָ בְּכֶתֶם אוֹפִיר at Ps 45:10, see now "Divine Queenship and Psalm 45" by B. L. Deal, a thesis available at this url pp. 14ff; also: T. Holm "Royal Women ... " in From Mari to Jerusalem and Back (Eisenbrauns, 2020), p. 155, n.23.
The usage as a divine name or epithet at Hatra is probably not to be connected with the divine name šngl at Teima, since we would expect to find the nun spelled in typical Hatran orthography.

entries.end_notes:
The word given as ܫܓܠܓܐ in BarB is cited by LS2 as a part of this entry, but the former gives a totally irrelevant meaning ("disagreement" or the like) and should be excluded. The easiest solution for this hapax is simply to emend away the shin and read ܓܠܠܐ.
For SL see now corrections: p. xv (cal.huc.edu/pdfs/corrsl2.pdf).

entries.end_notes: AIOA 102 suggests that the similar usage for a "chest" in NB is an Aramaic loan. CAD Š/2 p. 402 has now decided that the meaning of the Akkadian term is "stand (of wood or silver) for a large vat," but this has undoubtedly been influenced by the Aramaic usage although the single lexical list with the entry does seem to mean "base" for a beer vat. The BH hapax expression ‏שִׁדה וְשִׁדוֹת at Eccl. 2:8 (clearly as a double reading for שָׁרִים וְשָׁרוֹת) does not help resolve the possible history of this word. The definition "an earthen pot with two handles" cited as from the dialect of Takrit Psm" would seem to point in the right direction. Note especially in this regard the cited interpretation of IshGen.

entries.end_notes:
It is not correct to say that šwq can be "street" OR "market." In the Near East these have always been the same thing. Indeed all of the above glosses are essentially one thing. From Assyrian sūqu; see AIOA 93f.
For narrower town streets without market stalls see s.vv. brytˀ n.f. in the east and šqq n.m. and its derivatives quite widely attested (unsurprisingly, both are Akkadian loans as well) . For major streets and thoroughfares, usually outside of towns and cities the loanword ˀsṭrṭ is used in the west. Apparently, then, the only native Aramaic words in this semantic realm were such as ˀwrḥ, ˀwrḥˀ n.f. and šbyl, šbylˀ n.m. The ethnographic implications should be obvious.

entries.primary_gloss: small trace or piece of fiber
entries.end_notes: See Sh. Friedman, MAARAV 21(2014[2017]) p. 314 and now his "Elucidating Talmudic suryaqe—an Exercise in Talmudic Lexicography", pp. 383-408 in Hakol Kol Yaakov: The Joel Roth Jubilee Volume, ed. Robert A. Harris and Jonathan S. Milgram, where a convincing argument is made that the original talmudic meaning is not "streak" nor "vein", but simply "tiny strand, trace". The "heat or glare of the Sun" cited by BarBahlul for Syriac ܫܘܪܝܩܐ is clearly an Arabism from the common Arabic root for sunrise, east and has here been omitted. We assume that the correct Aramaic root is indeed srq, "to comb fabric".

entries.end_notes: The example for "death" is probably better emended to בשכבי, i.e. "among the dead" (from škb, škbˀn.m. : dead body, corpse).

DJBA provides an example of "sleeper" as the first meaning. This is incorrect, as the context is clearly "the living and the dead".
The assignment of the various unvocalized nominal examples to the proper noun formation is highly uncertain in some cases. Note that DJBA distinguishes between שֶׁכבא "sleeper, dead person" and שׁיכבא "lying down, death", even though the latter form may represent any of the homographs #1, #2, or #3, while the former form may be any at all, including #4. In Jewish texts, some of the forms with internal yodh vowel letter may have been incorrectly influenced by such forms as שכיבה and the thematic vowel of the verb itself.

entries.form_section: A highly irregular verb. The initial aleph is prothetic, but the resulting form is treated as a quasi-afel, often with a preformative vowel in a instead of original e. Hence the active participle in mem, already in OfA, which even shows the distinctive heh of the early h/afel. CPA shows a perfect passive )$kyx ; Sam. almost always $qx or $q( : Mand. T-stem as if true ettafal; LJLA regularly shows initial heh in perfect forms of the T-stem, reflecting the BA form.
entries.end_notes: The meaning "to be able" may be a devlopment from the earlier expression in the same meaning with "hand, power" as the subject; see s.v. yd n.f.

entries.end_notes: The LS2-suggested Syriac vocalization as šakkīḇ must be seen as uncertain, as is the JBA vocalization: the Yemenite tradition has škiyb . Note the vocalization of the Syriac of škybw "death" with initial shwa. Logically, one might expect the reverse, since death is rather permanent whereas sleeping is not. The majority of Syriac p.p.-based adjectives have moved to geminated middle radical forms, however. In most such cases, one should assume that both forms were used in early times.

Levy suggests a comparison with šlṭwn #2 arguing that both refer to a type of design typical of shields. The obvious solution is that it refers to small shield- or quiver-shaped spots of the animal's coat, but it is vocalized differently from the other example of šlṭ.

entries.end_notes: Compare $lqyn.

In all dialects this lexeme can be used for "sending word," but in many it is properly kept distinct from terms for sending people or goods; see s.vv. yšr and šdr.
In some Palestinian texts and LJLA the pael is also used for simple sending, as shown in the citations, but the note in DJPA suggesting that all exxs. in PTA and Gal. are paels takes things too far, and indeed he misunderstands the function of pael in this verb. The examples he cites of vocalized forms in PTA are of the pael because they mean "to release" not because they mean "to send." In the simple meaning, all unvocalized forms must thus be taken to be of G and Gt.
While it is impossible to disambiguate between possible Gt and Dt forms in the former dialects, the literary dialects in contrast (i.e. JLA, Syr) clearly have only Gt for "to be sent on a mission".
In later dialects "stretching out the hand" is expressed with yšṭ C.

entries.form_section: OA: ˀšm; abs. JLAtg $OM, $EM ; ; CPA $ym ; Syr šem; pl. in -ahān and also -ē in Syriac and -awān in CPA. In Mandaic the first vowel /u/ is preserved in all forms.
entries.end_notes:
See also s.v. lšm prep., a usage often difficult to distinguish semantically from l_ prep. preceding this word.
We assume that in JLAtg there was at one time a distinction of a sort (i.e., idiolectal and/or between absolute and construct forms) among the three attested forms with short /o/,/u/, and /e/ vowels, but the versions of the texts at our disposal do not provide any clear evidence.

See also s.v. šmt and etymological discussion s.v. $mt n.m.
The common use of this verb in BH in the sense of "to destroy" demonstrates its common NW Semitic heritage, however the development into the concept of "excommunication" may be influenced by the Akkadian Št stem of emēdu "to get mixed up together", in this case, with the "other."

LS2 distinguishes between D and C examples of identical meaning, but there is no clear morphological evidence to justify such distinction.
Both JBA examples cited in the main body of the article in DJBA are better taken as adjectives (see šmyn). Only the dubious example in his note may be a verbal form, but it could also be an adjective.
The basic meaning usually conveys the ideas of good nourishment and health rather than the often negative connotation of "fat" in modern times.

entries.end_notes:
Note the unusual passive form יתשמע at Sf A1; possibly an early example if ittaf'al.
We have here listed the G p.p. construction as a separate gloss because with this verb it is by for the most common such construction, albeit a grammatical one rather than a lexical peculiarity, and because it has been the subject of some discussion. E.Y. Kutscher, in "Two 'Passive' Constructions..." (1969) argued that this construction was borrowed from Old Persian. Several more recent scholars, however, have realized that it is simply an example of the the middle stage of the common Semitic and Indo-European linguistic drift passive > perfect > preterite.

A date palm typically has many thin roots stretching away large distances, while the taproot is typically quite pointy, i.e., spit-like. Surely the distance they small roots spread and the soil beneath them is not meant to be included for subsequent plantings in the talmudic context.

Note that many lexx. separate the legal meaning of the pael from the primary physical meaning. Indeed, the pael sense "to pay off" seems to be ultimately dependent upon the uniquely Middle-Assyrian ŠD stem šuppû, "to transfer property," paralleled by the D stem stative uppû, "transferred." See CAD A vol. 2 : 202b.
Contra LS2 there is no certain example of a G finite form intransitive.

entries.end_notes:
LS2 incorrectly gives the meaning "shore" for the passage in Pesh. Gen. 49:13 where it does indeed translate BH חוף the second time it appears in the verse. It is rather to be understood as a word play on the similar sounding correct translation of חוף as ܣܦܪܐ earlier in the verse.
At Pesh. Is 21:4 the form (corresponding to MT נֶשֶׁף) must be emended to špr "dawn," for the scribe, failing to understand the metaphor, has inserted waw.
For Sam. see s.v. špyr adj.

entries.end_notes: Compare špr #1 vb. C: to cleanse, wipe off. The Mandaic usage in the G stem, probably a back formation, occurs only in Asfar Maluašia and differs from #1 in the thematic vowel of the G imperfect. MD suggests it is a borrowing from the rare classical Arabic šfr "to decline (said of the sun)", however it does not occur in just the latest portions of that text.

entries.end_notes: Note that Bar Hebraeus uses a masculine plural form (i.e. normally only the plural of šrb #2 "narrative") for the sense of "genus". (The form ܫܪܒ at BHde Plantis 109:393, however, clearly means just "subject". Absent a complete concordance of his writings, though, it may be best to assume that he uses the two forms interchangeably. But the sense "family, clan" is now possibly attested for the masculine plural form in Old Aramaic as well. In Mandaic a masculine singular form of šrb#2 has clearly been developed through back-formation (even though such a masculine plural ending on either form is not otherwise attested!

entries.form_section: In JBA the afel may have developed into an independent verb, to judge by the spellings with preserved aleph in the participle and imperfect, resulting in a form looking like a D stem. See DJBA s.v. )$r #2 vb. and s.v. ˁšr, but note that similar spellings also occur in Syriac.
entries.end_notes: Jastrow mistakenly takes the Rabbinic Hebrew expression ישר כחך = Aramaic ישר חילך "well done, congratulations" to be from the Hebrew root ישר, a blatantly incorrect folk etymology, itself probably influenced by it pronunciation in Yiddish.

The figurative usage "to be irrigated" may well be due to Akkadian; see CAD Š/2 p. 215.
Given that what looks like a C form in CPA is found only in the late LN text and only as a participle, it is probably only an unusual late G form much as the G of the phonologically similar root škḥ becomes confused with C, although in that case it is a early and common Aramaic development.

Nonetheless, by those later times the semantic distinction was clearly no longer strictly maintained, if at all.
